title: Cancer Cell Biology
authors: Jeffrey W. Clark, Dan L. Longo

items.title: How cancers are defined and named by tissue of origin
items.content:
Cancer is defined by a set of cellular behaviors.
Cancers are characterized by unregulated cell division, avoidance of cell death, tissue invasion, and the ability to metastasize; a neoplasm is benign when it grows in an unregulated fashion without invasion or metastasis.
Cancers are named by tissue of origin: those from epithelial tissue are carcinomas, from mesenchymal tissue are sarcomas, and from hematopoietic tissue are leukemias, lymphomas, and plasma cell dyscrasias (including multiple myeloma).
Cancers arise from genetic alterations, the vast majority beginning in a single cell and therefore monoclonal in origin, though they become markedly heterogeneous over time.
keyPoints: Malignancy = unregulated growth + invasion + metastasis (benign lacks invasion/metastasis)
Epithelial → carcinoma; mesenchymal → sarcoma; hematopoietic → leukemia/lymphoma/myeloma
Monoclonal origin but marked heterogeneity and plasticity develop over time
items.reference: CANCER CELL BIOLOGY: "those derived from epithelial tissue are called carcinomas, those derived from mesenchymal tissues are sarcomas, and those derived from hematopoietic tissue are leukemias, lymphomas, and plasma cell dyscrasias (including multiple myeloma)."

items.title: How most cancers develop through a multistep progression
items.content:
While a few cancers are driven primarily by a single dominant oncogene, most are multistep.
Most human cancers involve many genetic abnormalities, each contributing to loss of control of proliferation/differentiation and to acquired capabilities such as tissue invasion, metastasis, angiogenesis, and alteration of the extracellular environment.
Many cancers progress through recognizable phenotypes: hyperplasia → adenoma → dysplasia → carcinoma in situ → invasive cancer with the ability to metastasize, usually over many years.
Normal cells have extensive safeguards (DNA repair, DNA-damage responses) against the damage that these steps overcome.
keyPoints: A few cancers are single-oncogene-driven (e.g., CML/abl, ~half of melanomas/braf), but most are multistep
Progression: hyperplasia → adenoma → dysplasia → carcinoma in situ → invasive cancer
The process usually takes many years and overcomes normal DNA-repair safeguards
items.reference: CANCER CELL BIOLOGY: "Many cancers go through recognizable steps of progressively more abnormal phenotypes: hyperplasia, to adenoma, to dysplasia, to carcinoma in situ, to invasive cancer with the ability to metastasize."

items.title: Why oncogenes and tumor-suppressor genes affect cancer in opposite ways
items.content:
Two opposite classes of genes drive deregulated proliferation.
Oncogenes are genes that can promote cell growth when altered (e.g., Ras, Myc); they were first identified as captured, mutated normal cellular genes in tumor-causing animal viruses.
Tumor-suppressor genes (e.g., Rb, p53) normally inhibit cancer development or growth, so their loss of function permits malignant behavior.
Deregulated proliferation in cancer combines increased action of positive growth regulators (oncogenes) with loss of function of negative growth regulators (tumor suppressors).
keyPoints: Oncogenes (Ras, Myc) promote growth when activated — gain of function
Tumor-suppressor genes (Rb, p53) restrain growth — loss of function enables cancer
Cancer typically combines activated oncogenes with inactivated suppressors
items.reference: TABLE 77-1: "Loss of function of negative growth regulators (tumor suppressor genes, i.e., Rb, p53) and increased action of positive growth regulators (oncogenes, i.e., Ras, Myc)."

items.title: How Rb controls the G1/S checkpoint
items.content:
Entry into DNA synthesis is gated at the G1/S checkpoint, with Rb as the brake.
In G1 the cell verifies it is ready to replicate (repair machinery, replicating enzymes, nucleotides, energy); the retinoblastoma protein Rb places a brake on progression until the cell is ready.
When ready, sequential activation of cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) phosphorylates and inactivates Rb, which releases the transcription factor E2F/DP1 so S-phase genes are expressed; CDK inhibitors such as p21, p16INK4a, and p27 can block the CDKs if the cell is not ready.
Nearly every cancer has one or more defects in the G1 checkpoint, permitting progression to S phase despite DNA-repair abnormalities.
keyPoints: Rb is the brake at G1/S; CDK phosphorylation inactivates it, releasing E2F to drive S-phase genes
CDK inhibitors (p21, p16INK4a, p27) hold the cell if it is not ready
Nearly every cancer has a G1-checkpoint defect
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "Nearly every cancer has one or more defects in the G1 checkpoint that permit progression to S phase despite abnormalities in DNA repair machinery..."

items.title: Why p53 is called the guardian of the genome
items.content:
p53 is the master sensor that prevents propagation of damaged cells.
Normally present at very low levels (bound to the ubiquitin ligase mdm2, which both inhibits and degrades it), p53 is stabilized when DNA damage activates the ATM pathway, which phosphorylates mdm2 and releases p53.
Freed p53 stops cell-cycle progression, directs synthesis of repair enzymes, or — if damage is too great — initiates apoptosis to prevent propagation of a damaged cell.
Mutations in p53 (chromosome 17p) are among the most frequent in human cancers; germline mutation causes Li-Fraumeni syndrome, and HPV degrades p53 via its E6 oncoprotein.
keyPoints: p53 is held low by mdm2; DNA damage (via ATM) releases it
p53 → cell-cycle arrest, DNA repair, or apoptosis if damage is severe
Among the most commonly mutated genes; germline mutation = Li-Fraumeni syndrome
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "Called the guardian of the genome, p53 is a transcription factor that is normally present in the cell in very low levels...p53 then stops cell cycle progression, directs the synthesis of repair enzymes, or if the damage is too great, initiates apoptosis..."

items.title: How HPV transforms cells by inactivating p53 and Rb
items.content:
The importance of p53 and Rb is underscored by how a common oncogenic virus works.
Human papillomavirus has two main oncogenes, E6 and E7: E6 increases the rapid turnover (degradation) of p53, and E7 inhibits Rb function.
Inhibition of these two tumor-suppressor pathways is required for transformation of epithelial cells by the virus.
This shows that simultaneously disabling the p53 and Rb checkpoints — whether by mutation or by viral proteins — is central to malignant transformation.
keyPoints: HPV E6 → degrades p53; HPV E7 → inhibits Rb
Inactivating both is required for HPV-mediated epithelial transformation
Illustrates the centrality of the p53 and Rb checkpoints to cancer
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "This virus has two main oncogenes, E6 and E7. E6 acts to increase the rapid turnover of p53, and E7 acts to inhibit Rb function; inhibition of these two targets is required for transformation of epithelial cells by the virus."

items.title: Why spindle-checkpoint failure produces aneuploidy
items.content:
A third checkpoint during mitosis guards chromosome number.
The spindle checkpoint (in M phase) ensures proper attachment of chromosomes to the mitotic spindle before the cell divides; if alignment is wrong, chromosome number is abnormal, or centromeres are improperly paired, the cell initiates death to prevent aneuploid progeny.
Abnormalities in the spindle checkpoint facilitate the development of aneuploidy (an altered number of chromosomes), which is frequently found in cancers and is a predominant genetic feature of some tumors.
In general, tumors have either defects in chromosome number (chromosomal instability) or defective DNA repair pathways, but not both.
keyPoints: Spindle checkpoint ensures correct chromosome-spindle attachment before division
Its failure permits aneuploidy, common in cancers
Tumors usually have chromosomal instability OR defective DNA repair, not both
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "Abnormalities in the spindle checkpoint facilitate the development of aneuploidy, which is frequently found in cancers."

items.title: How telomerase reactivation grants cancer cells replicative immortality
items.content:
Normal cells have a built-in replication limit that cancer cells escape.
Normal cells stop dividing after a limited number of doublings (replicative senescence), in part because DNA polymerase cannot replicate chromosome tips, so telomeres shorten with each division until a p53-regulated DNA-damage response triggers arrest.
Cells that bypass this limit (e.g., with nonfunctional pRb and p53) survive crisis and reactivate telomerase — an enzyme with a reverse-transcriptase subunit (hTERT) and an RNA template that adds TTAGGG repeats to chromosome ends.
More than 90% of human cancers express high levels of telomerase, allowing indefinite proliferation; inhibiting it causes tumor-cell apoptosis in vitro, though normal stem cells also require telomerase.
keyPoints: Telomeres shorten each division → senescence/crisis (a tumor-suppressive limit)
>90% of cancers reactivate telomerase (hTERT) to maintain telomeres and divide indefinitely
Telomerase inhibition is a target, but normal stem cells also depend on telomerase
items.reference: CELLULAR SENESCENCE: "More than 90% of human cancers express high levels of telomerase that prevent telomere shortening to critical levels and allow indefinite cell proliferation."

items.title: How kinase-driven signaling pathways are targeted in cancer
items.content:
Cancers hijack reversible phosphorylation cascades for proliferation and survival.
Kinases add phosphate groups (tyrosine kinases, serine/threonine kinases; some like MEK do both) and phosphatases remove them; two key pathways are RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK and the PI3K pathway.
In many cancers receptor tyrosine kinases or downstream components are activated by mutation, amplification, or translocation to have prolonged activity, making them therapeutic targets.
Examples: EGFR-mutant lung cancers respond to osimertinib; BRAF (a serine/threonine kinase) inhibitors are effective in melanoma/thyroid cancer (combined BRAF+MEK inhibition is better); imatinib targets BCR-Abl in CML, c-Kit and PDGFRα in GIST.
keyPoints: Key oncogenic pathways: RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K
RTKs/components activated by mutation, amplification, or translocation → druggable targets
Osimertinib (EGFR lung), BRAF±MEK inhibitors (melanoma), imatinib (BCR-Abl CML, c-Kit/PDGFRα GIST)
items.reference: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS: "Two important signaling pathways are the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK pathway and the phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase (PI3K) pathway."

items.title: Why 'oncogene addiction' makes targeted therapy work
items.content:
Some tumors become dependent on a single activated pathway.
Oncogene addiction occurs when a tumor cell develops an activating mutation in an oncogene that becomes the dominant pathway for survival and growth, with reduced contributions from other pathways.
This dependency creates a cell that is vulnerable to inhibitors of that oncogene pathway — for example, BRAF-mutant cells are sensitive to MEK inhibitors that block BRAF-pathway signaling.
However, no targeted therapy has yet been curative alone, and resistance almost always eventually develops (e.g., the BCR-Abl T315I mutation prevents imatinib binding).
keyPoints: Oncogene addiction = dependence on one activated pathway → vulnerability to its inhibitor
Basis of targeted therapy (e.g., BRAF-mutant cells sensitive to MEK inhibition)
Resistance almost always develops (e.g., BCR-Abl T315I blocks imatinib)
items.reference: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS: "This dependency on a single pathway creates a cell that is vulnerable to inhibitors of that oncogene pathway."

items.title: How synthetic lethality lets PARP inhibitors target BRCA-mutant cancers
items.content:
Synthetic lethality exploits a tumor's DNA-repair defect rather than repairing it.
Synthetic lethality occurs when loss of function in either of two genes alone has limited effect on survival, but loss of both leads to cell death.
Cells with BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations cannot repair DNA damage by homologous recombination; PARP is essential for single-strand-break repair, so PARP inhibition selectively kills BRCA-deficient cancer cells.
This is far more tractable than trying to restore a mutated tumor suppressor, and PARP inhibitors are approved for BRCA-mutated ovarian, breast, and pancreatic cancers (and likely other DNA-repair-deficient tumors).
keyPoints: Synthetic lethality: losing either gene alone is tolerated, losing both is lethal
BRCA-mutant cells lack homologous recombination → depend on PARP-mediated repair
PARP inhibition selectively kills BRCA-deficient cells (approved in ovarian/breast/pancreatic cancer)
items.reference: CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY: "PARP inhibition results in selective killing of cancer cells that have lost BRCA1 or BRCA2 function."

items.title: Why promoter hypermethylation silences tumor-suppressor genes
items.content:
Epigenetic change can inactivate a gene without altering its DNA sequence.
Epigenetic changes alter gene expression across at least one cell division without changing the DNA code — including cytosine (CpG) methylation, histone acetylation/methylation, and higher-order chromatin changes.
Hypermethylation of DNA promoter regions is a common mechanism by which tumor-suppressor loci are epigenetically silenced; one allele may be inactivated by mutation/deletion while the other is silenced by methylation, causing loss of function.
Unlike DNA mutations, epigenetic changes are potentially reversible and amenable to therapy (e.g., silenced VHL, BRCA1, MLH1; demethylating agents like 5-aza-2-deoxycytidine in myelodysplastic syndrome).
keyPoints: Epigenetic = heritable expression change without DNA-sequence change
Promoter hypermethylation silences tumor suppressors (often the 'second hit')
Reversible → targetable (demethylating agents, HDAC inhibitors)
items.reference: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION: "Hypermethylation of DNA promoter regions is a common mechanism by which tumor-suppressor loci are epigenetically silenced in cancer cells."

items.title: How PML-RARα and ATRA exemplify reversing an epigenetic block
items.content:
Acute promyelocytic leukemia is the model of epigenetic, differentiation-based therapy.
The PML-RARα fusion protein, generated by the t(15;17) translocation in most acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), binds retinoic-acid-response-element promoters and recruits histone deacetylases (HDACs), inhibiting gene expression and arresting differentiation at the promyelocyte stage.
Pharmacologic all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA), the ligand for RARα, releases HDAC activity and recruits coactivators, overcoming the differentiation block.
ATRA represents a treatment paradigm for the reversal of epigenetic changes in cancer; several HDAC inhibitors are approved (e.g., for cutaneous/peripheral T-cell lymphoma and multiple myeloma).
keyPoints: APL: t(15;17) PML-RARα recruits HDACs → differentiation block at promyelocyte stage
ATRA releases HDACs and restores differentiation — a paradigm for reversing epigenetic change
HDAC inhibitors (vorinostat, romidepsin, belinostat, panobinostat) are approved for some cancers
items.reference: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION: "Treatment with pharmacologic doses of all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA), the ligand for RARα, results in the release of HDAC activity and the recruitment of coactivators, which overcome the differentiation block."

items.title: How the extrinsic and intrinsic apoptosis pathways converge on caspases
items.content:
Programmed cell death proceeds by two main pathways feeding effector caspases.
The extrinsic pathway is activated by cross-linking TNF-receptor-superfamily death receptors (CD95/Fas, DR4/DR5) by their ligands, recruiting FADD and procaspase-8 to activate effector caspases-3 and -7.
The intrinsic pathway releases cytochrome c and SMAC from mitochondria in response to DNA damage, loss of ECM adherence, oncogene-induced proliferation, or growth-factor deprivation, activating caspase-9; the balance of antiapoptotic Bcl-2-family members (Bcl-2, Bcl-XL, Mcl-1) versus proapoptotic BH3-only proteins governs mitochondrial pore formation.
Cancers subvert apoptosis (e.g., Bcl-2 overexpression from t(14;18) in follicular lymphoma), which also makes them vulnerable to BH3-mimetics such as venetoclax.
keyPoints: Extrinsic: death receptors (Fas/DR4/DR5) → FADD/caspase-8 → caspase-3/7
Intrinsic: mitochondrial cytochrome c/SMAC → caspase-9; balanced by Bcl-2 vs BH3-only proteins
Bcl-2 overexpression (t(14;18)) blocks apoptosis; venetoclax (BH3-mimetic) targets it in CLL/AML
items.reference: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH: "An oral BH3 mimetic inhibitor of BCL-2, venetoclax, is approved for use in patients with refractory CLL with 17p deletion, and is active in AML."

items.title: Why Gompertzian growth means tumors slow their own growth
items.content:
Tumor growth is not exponential, which has therapeutic implications.
Most cancers become clinically detectable at ~1 cm (≈10^9 cells), often present at ~10^10 cells, and reach a lethal burden at ~10^12–10^13 cells.
Human tumors grow by Gompertzian kinetics — not every daughter cell keeps dividing — so the growth fraction declines with time: it is 100% for the first malignant cell but estimated to be <5% by the time a patient presents.
This presentation growth fraction is similar to that of normal bone marrow and intestinal epithelium, explaining the dose-limiting toxicities of agents that target dividing cells.
keyPoints: Tumor burden: detectable ~1 cm/10^9 cells; lethal ~10^12–10^13 cells
Gompertzian kinetics: growth fraction falls from 100% (first cell) to <5% at presentation
Similar growth fraction to marrow/gut epithelium → dose-limiting toxicity of cytotoxics
items.reference: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS: "The growth fraction of the first malignant cell is 100%, and by the time a patient presents for medical care, the growth fraction is estimated to be <5%..."

items.title: How tumor cells complete the metastatic cascade
items.content:
Metastasis accounts for the vast majority of deaths from solid tumors and requires multiple steps.
The initial step is migration and invasion through the extracellular matrix — adhesion to the basement membrane, local proteolysis, and movement through the membrane and ECM.
Cells that lose ECM contact normally undergo anoikis (apoptosis from loss of contact), which must be suppressed in metastasizing cells, and many epithelial cancers undergo epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) to gain motility while retaining proliferative capacity.
The rate-limiting step is the ability of tumor cells to survive and expand in the novel microenvironment of the metastatic site while evading host defenses including the immune system.
keyPoints: Invasion = basement-membrane adhesion + local proteolysis + migration through ECM
Metastasizing cells suppress anoikis and often undergo EMT (motile mesenchymal phenotype)
Rate-limiting step: surviving and expanding in the foreign metastatic niche
items.reference: METASTASIS: "The rate-limiting step for metastasis is the ability for tumor cells to survive and expand in the novel microenvironment of the metastatic site..."

items.title: How bone metastases exploit the RANK/RANKL/OPG axis
items.content:
Bone metastases hijack normal bone-remodeling signaling.
Osteoclasts (and their precursors) express RANK, required for their differentiation and activation; osteoblasts/stromal cells express RANK ligand (RANKL), and osteoprotegerin (OPG) is a decoy receptor that inhibits RANK activation — the RANKL/OPG balance sets osteoclast activity.
Many tumors increase osteoclast activity by secreting PTH, PTH-related peptide, IL-1, or Mip1, tipping the balance toward bone resorption (e.g., multiple myeloma's lytic lesions).
Inhibition of RANKL by the antibody denosumab can prevent further bone destruction, and bisphosphonates also inhibit osteoclast function in patients with bone metastases.
keyPoints: RANK (osteoclasts) / RANKL (osteoblasts) / OPG (decoy) balance controls bone resorption
Tumors secrete PTHrP, IL-1, etc. to drive osteoclast-mediated bone destruction (e.g., myeloma)
Denosumab (anti-RANKL) and bisphosphonates inhibit osteoclasts in bone metastases
items.reference: METASTASIS: "Inhibition of RANKL by an antibody (denosumab) can prevent further bone destruction. Bisphosphonates are also effective inhibitors of osteoclast function..."

items.title: Why cancer cells use aerobic glycolysis (the Warburg effect)
items.content:
Cancer metabolism is reprogrammed to build new cells, not just make energy.
Many cancer cells use aerobic glycolysis (the Warburg effect) to metabolize glucose, producing increased lactic acid, whereas normal cells use the more ATP-efficient oxidative phosphorylation — but glycolysis better supplies the building blocks needed for new cells.
A consequence is increased glucose uptake, exploited clinically by FDG-PET scanning to detect tumors.
Mutations in metabolic genes also occur: IDH1/IDH2 mutations (gliomas, AML, cholangiocarcinoma) produce the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate, which alters DNA methylation; IDH inhibitors are approved for IDH-mutant disease.
keyPoints: Warburg effect: aerobic glycolysis (↑lactate) provides biosynthetic building blocks
↑Glucose uptake underlies FDG-PET tumor detection
IDH1/2 mutations make 2-hydroxyglutarate (oncometabolite); IDH inhibitors approved (AML, cholangiocarcinoma, glioma)
items.reference: CANCER METABOLISM: "Many cancer cells utilize aerobic glycolysis (the Warburg effect)...One consequence is increased glucose uptake and utilization by cancer cells, a fact utilized in fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG)-positron emission tomography (PET) scanning to detect tumors."

items.title: How tumors switch on angiogenesis and how it is targeted
items.content:
Growth beyond a small size requires new blood vessels via the 'angiogenic switch'.
The angiogenic switch is the phase when the balance of pro- and antiangiogenic factors tips toward vessel formation; stimuli include hypoxia, inflammation, and oncogene/tumor-suppressor lesions, and most tumor vessels arise by sprouting driven by VEGFs.
Hypoxia-inducible factors (HIF-1, HIF-2) are central, stimulating VEGF and VEGF-receptor transcription (HIF-2 has the bigger angiogenic role); VHL loss (e.g., in RCC) stabilizes HIF.
Bevacizumab (an anti-VEGF antibody) modestly potentiates chemotherapy in several cancers, and VEGF-pathway inhibitors are especially effective in renal cell cancer; hypertension is the most common side effect.
keyPoints: Angiogenic switch tips pro-/antiangiogenic balance toward vessel formation (mostly VEGF-driven sprouting)
HIF-1/HIF-2 drive VEGF transcription; VHL loss stabilizes HIF (RCC)
Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) and VEGF-pathway TKIs (effective in RCC); hypertension is the commonest toxicity
items.reference: MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION: "most tumor blood vessels arise by the process of sprouting, in which tumors secrete trophic angiogenic molecules, the most potent being vascular endothelial growth factors (VEGFs)..."

items.title: How cancers evade the immune system and how checkpoint inhibitors counter it
items.content:
The immune system normally restrains cancer, and tumors evolve to escape it.
Immunosuppressed individuals (inherited immune defects, transplant immunosuppression, HIV) have increased cancer risk, underscoring immune surveillance.
Cancers evade detection by downregulating MHC and tumor antigens, expressing inhibitory ligands (e.g., PD-L1), secreting immunosuppressive factors (TGF-β), recruiting regulatory T cells, and inducing T-cell tolerance.
Immunotherapy either activates the immune response or releases its brakes: checkpoint inhibitors against CTLA-4, PD-1, and PD-L1 are approved across many cancers; CAR-T cells (e.g., anti-CD19) achieve durable remissions in ALL/DLBCL; tumors with high mutational burden or microsatellite instability respond best.
keyPoints: Immune surveillance is real — immunosuppression raises cancer risk
Evasion: ↓MHC/antigens, PD-L1, TGF-β, regulatory T cells, T-cell tolerance
Checkpoint inhibitors (CTLA-4/PD-1/PD-L1) and CAR-T (anti-CD19) work; high TMB/MSI predict response
items.reference: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS: "These include downregulation of cell surface proteins involved in immune recognition...expression of other cell surface proteins that inhibit immune function (including members of the B7 family of proteins such as PD-L1)..."

items.question: A malignant tumor arising from epithelial tissue is correctly termed a:
items.explanation: Cancers are named by tissue of origin: epithelial → carcinoma, mesenchymal → sarcoma, and hematopoietic → leukemia/lymphoma/plasma cell dyscrasias.
items.reference: CANCER CELL BIOLOGY: "those derived from epithelial tissue are called carcinomas, those derived from mesenchymal tissues are sarcomas..."

items.question: p53 and Rb are best classified as:
options: Tumor-suppressor genes that normally inhibit cancer growth
items.explanation: p53 and Rb are tumor-suppressor genes; when functioning normally they inhibit the development or growth of cancer, so their loss of function permits malignancy.
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "Critical proteins in these control processes that are frequently mutated or otherwise inactivated in cancers are called tumor-suppressor genes...Examples include p53 and Rb."

items.question: Phosphorylation of the retinoblastoma protein (Rb) by cyclin-dependent kinases promotes cell-cycle progression by:
options: Releasing the transcription factor E2F/DP1 to express S-phase genes
items.explanation: CDK-mediated phosphorylation inactivates Rb (the brake), which releases E2F/DP1 so genes required for S-phase progression are expressed.
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "Phosphorylated Rb releases the S phase-regulating transcription factor, E2F/DP1, and genes required for S-phase progression are expressed."

items.question: Under normal conditions, p53 is maintained at low levels because it is bound by which protein that targets it for degradation?
items.explanation: p53 is normally bound to mdm2, a ubiquitin ligase that inhibits p53 transcriptional activity and targets it for proteasomal degradation; DNA damage activates ATM, which phosphorylates mdm2 and releases p53.
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "Normally, p53 is bound to mdm2, a ubiquitin ligase that both inhibits p53 transcriptional activation and also targets p53 for degradation in the proteasome."

items.question: The HPV E6 and E7 oncoproteins transform epithelial cells by, respectively:
options: Increasing p53 turnover and inhibiting Rb function
items.explanation: HPV E6 increases the rapid turnover (degradation) of p53, and E7 inhibits Rb function; inactivating both tumor suppressors is required for transformation.
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "E6 acts to increase the rapid turnover of p53, and E7 acts to inhibit Rb function..."

items.question: Approximately what proportion of human cancers express high levels of telomerase to maintain telomeres and allow indefinite proliferation?
items.explanation: More than 90% of human cancers express high levels of telomerase, which prevents critical telomere shortening and permits indefinite proliferation.
items.reference: CELLULAR SENESCENCE: "More than 90% of human cancers express high levels of telomerase..."

items.question: Imatinib is effective against chronic myeloid leukemia because it targets which kinase generated by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation?
items.explanation: Imatinib targets the BCR-Abl tyrosine kinase produced by the t(9;22) translocation in CML; it also inhibits c-Kit (GIST) and PDGFRα. Resistance can arise from the T315I ATP-pocket mutation.
items.reference: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS: "Imatinib (which targets a number of tyrosine kinases) is an effective agent in tumors that have translocations of the c-Abl and BCR gene (such as chronic myeloid leukemia)..."

items.question: The BCR-Abl T315I mutation causes resistance to imatinib because it:
options: Alters the ATP-binding pocket so the inhibitor cannot bind
items.explanation: Many kinase inhibitors are competitive inhibitors of the ATP-binding pocket; the T315I (threonine-to-isoleucine) change in that pocket prevents imatinib binding. (Ponatinib retains activity against T315I.)
items.reference: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS: "mutation in the critical BCR-ABL kinase in the ATP-binding pocket (such as the threonine to isoleucine change at codon 315 [T315I]) can prevent imatinib binding."

items.question: PARP inhibitors selectively kill tumor cells with BRCA1/2 mutations through which principle?
items.explanation: BRCA-mutant cells cannot repair DNA by homologous recombination; blocking PARP (needed for single-strand-break repair) is lethal only in combination with the BRCA defect — synthetic lethality.
items.reference: CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY: "PARP inhibition results in selective killing of cancer cells that have lost BRCA1 or BRCA2 function."

items.question: A common epigenetic mechanism for inactivating a tumor-suppressor gene's remaining allele is:
options: Point mutation in the coding region
items.explanation: Hypermethylation of DNA promoter regions epigenetically silences tumor-suppressor loci; commonly one allele is inactivated by mutation/deletion while the other is silenced by methylation.
items.reference: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION: "Hypermethylation of DNA promoter regions is a common mechanism by which tumor-suppressor loci are epigenetically silenced..."

items.question: All-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) treats acute promyelocytic leukemia by overcoming a differentiation block caused by the PML-RARα fusion protein. ATRA works by:
options: Releasing recruited HDAC activity and recruiting coactivators to restore differentiation
items.explanation: PML-RARα recruits HDACs to retinoic-acid-response-element promoters, blocking differentiation; pharmacologic ATRA releases HDAC activity and recruits coactivators, overcoming the block — a paradigm for reversing epigenetic change.
items.reference: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION: "Treatment with pharmacologic doses of all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA)...results in the release of HDAC activity and the recruitment of coactivators, which overcome the differentiation block."

items.question: Venetoclax treats CLL and AML by acting as a BH3 mimetic that inhibits which antiapoptotic protein?
items.explanation: Venetoclax is an oral BH3 mimetic that inhibits BCL-2, an antiapoptotic Bcl-2-family protein overexpressed in many lymphoid malignancies, thereby promoting apoptosis.
items.reference: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH: "An oral BH3 mimetic inhibitor of BCL-2, venetoclax, is approved for use in patients with refractory CLL with 17p deletion, and is active in AML."

items.question: The intrinsic (mitochondrial) apoptosis pathway is initiated by:
options: Cross-linking of Fas (CD95) by Fas ligand
Release of cytochrome c and SMAC from mitochondria
items.explanation: The intrinsic pathway is initiated by release of cytochrome c and SMAC from the mitochondrial intermembrane space (in response to DNA damage, loss of ECM adherence, etc.), activating caspase-9. Fas cross-linking initiates the extrinsic pathway.
items.reference: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH: "The intrinsic pathway of apoptosis is initiated by the release of cytochrome c and SMAC...from the mitochondrial intermembrane space..."

items.question: By the time a patient presents with cancer, the tumor's growth fraction is estimated to be approximately:
items.explanation: Human tumors grow by Gompertzian kinetics; the growth fraction is 100% for the first malignant cell but declines to an estimated <5% by the time a patient presents — similar to normal marrow and gut epithelium.
items.reference: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS: "by the time a patient presents for medical care, the growth fraction is estimated to be <5%..."

items.question: The form of apoptosis induced when a cell loses contact with the extracellular matrix, which metastasizing cells must suppress, is termed:
items.explanation: Anoikis is apoptosis induced by loss of ECM contact; cells that metastasize must suppress anoikis to survive after detaching and traveling to distant sites.
items.reference: METASTASIS: "Cells that lose contact with the ECM normally undergo programmed cell death (anoikis-apoptosis induced by the loss of contact), and this process has to be suppressed in cells that metastasize."

items.question: Denosumab limits bone destruction from metastases by inhibiting:
items.explanation: Denosumab is an antibody against RANKL, blocking RANK activation of osteoclasts and thus preventing further osteoclast-mediated bone destruction; bisphosphonates similarly inhibit osteoclasts.
items.reference: METASTASIS: "Inhibition of RANKL by an antibody (denosumab) can prevent further bone destruction."

items.question: The increased glucose uptake that characterizes cancer cells (the Warburg effect) is clinically exploited by which imaging technique?
items.explanation: Aerobic glycolysis increases glucose uptake by cancer cells, which is the basis for fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG)-PET scanning to detect tumors.
items.reference: CANCER METABOLISM: "a fact utilized in fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG)-positron emission tomography (PET) scanning to detect tumors."

items.question: Which transcription factors are central to the angiogenic response, stimulating VEGF transcription especially under hypoxia?
items.explanation: Hypoxia-inducible factors (especially HIF-1 and HIF-2) stimulate transcription of hypoxia-responsive genes including VEGF and VEGF receptors; HIF-2 has the larger angiogenic role. VHL loss (e.g., in RCC) stabilizes HIF.
items.reference: MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION: "Central to the angiogenic response are hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs; especially 1 and 2)..."

items.question: What is the most common side effect of inhibitors of VEGF or its receptors?
items.explanation: Hypertension is the most common side effect of VEGF (or VEGFR) inhibitors and can usually be managed with antihypertensives; rarer serious risks include arterial thromboembolism, hemorrhage, bowel perforation, and impaired wound healing.
items.reference: ANTIANGIOGENIC THERAPY: "Hypertension is the most common side effect of inhibitors of VEGF (or its receptors)..."

items.question: Tumor characteristics associated with a better response to immune checkpoint inhibitors include:
options: Low tumor mutational burden and microsatellite stability
High tumor mutational burden and microsatellite instability (MSI-high)
items.explanation: High tumor mutational burden and microsatellite instability (with increased neoantigens), along with PD-L1 expression and tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte density, are associated with better responses to checkpoint inhibitors; MSI-stable pancreatic and colon cancers respond poorly.
items.reference: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS: "These include the number of mutations present in the tumor (tumor mutational burden), presence of increased neoantigens, expression of immune checkpoint proteins...density of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes..."

items.statement: The vast majority of cancers begin in a single cell and are therefore monoclonal in origin.
items.explanation: True — cancers arise from genetic alterations that the vast majority of the time begin in a single cell (monoclonal origin), though they become markedly heterogeneous over time.
items.reference: CANCER CELL BIOLOGY: "the vast majority of which begin in a single cell and therefore are monoclonal in origin."

items.statement: Nearly every cancer has one or more defects in the G1 checkpoint.
items.explanation: True — nearly every cancer has G1-checkpoint defects that permit progression to S phase despite DNA-repair abnormalities or other deficiencies.
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "Nearly every cancer has one or more defects in the G1 checkpoint..."

items.statement: Tumors typically have both chromosomal-number defects (aneuploidy) and defective DNA-repair pathways simultaneously.
items.explanation: False — in general, tumors have either defects in chromosome number (chromosomal instability) OR defective DNA repair pathways, but not both.
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "In general, tumors have either defects in chromosome number or defective DNA repair pathways but not both."

items.statement: Most normal somatic cells express enough telomerase to prevent telomere shortening with each division.
items.explanation: False — most normal somatic cells do NOT express sufficient telomerase to prevent telomere attrition; exceptions are stem cells (hematopoietic, gut/skin epithelium, germ cells). More than 90% of cancers reactivate telomerase.
items.reference: CELLULAR SENESCENCE: "Most normal somatic cells do not express sufficient telomerase to prevent telomere attrition with each cell division."

items.statement: Targeted kinase inhibitors have proven curative by themselves for several advanced malignancies.
items.explanation: False — none of these targeted therapies has yet been curative by itself for any malignancy, although prolonged disease control occurs (e.g., >80% 10-year survival in CML), and resistance ultimately develops in most patients.
items.reference: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS: "none of these targeted therapies has yet been curative by themselves for any malignancy..."

items.statement: Mutations in the ras family (especially KRAS) are the most common oncogene mutations in cancers but have been difficult drug targets.
items.explanation: True — ras mutations (especially kras) are the most common oncogene mutations and have been very difficult targets; agents against KRAS G12C are now approved for some lung cancers.
items.reference: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS: "Mutations in ras are the most common mutations in oncogenes in cancers (especially kras) but have proved to be very difficult targets..."

items.statement: Unlike DNA mutations, epigenetic changes are potentially reversible and amenable to therapeutic intervention.
items.explanation: True — unlike genetic events that alter DNA primary structure, epigenetic changes are potentially reversible and appear amenable to therapy (e.g., HDAC inhibitors, demethylating agents).
items.reference: EPIGENETIC INFLUENCES ON CANCER GENE TRANSCRIPTION: "Unlike genetic events that alter DNA primary structure (e.g., deletions), epigenetic changes are potentially reversible and appear amenable to therapeutic intervention."

items.statement: Overexpression of Bcl-2 from the t(14;18) translocation contributes to follicular lymphoma by blocking apoptosis.
items.explanation: True — Bcl-2 overexpression from t(14;18) is antiapoptotic and contributes to follicular lymphoma; this dependence also makes such tumors vulnerable to BH3-mimetics like venetoclax.
items.reference: APOPTOSIS AND OTHER MECHANISMS OF CELL DEATH: "overexpression of Bcl-2 as a result of the t(14;18) translocation contributes to follicular lymphoma..."

items.statement: Only a small proportion of the cells within a tumor are capable of initiating colonies or forming tumors efficiently when transplanted.
items.explanation: True — only a small proportion of tumor cells (e.g., <1% in AML/CML) have stem-cell-like properties of self-renewal and efficient tumor initiation, and their slow cycling and drug-efflux pumps make them resistant to therapy.
items.reference: CANCER STEM CELLS: "only a small proportion of the cells within a tumor are capable of initiating colonies in vitro or forming tumors at high efficiency when injected into immunocompromised NOD/SCID mice."

items.statement: Circulating tumor DNA can be analyzed from blood to identify targetable mutations and monitor therapy noninvasively.
items.explanation: True — as cancers grow, dying cells release DNA into the circulation; sensitive methods detect mutations in this circulating tumor DNA, offering a noninvasive way to identify targets, monitor response, and detect resistance or recurrence.
items.reference: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS: "An alternative approach to testing samples directly from tumors is to test blood for the presence of mutations or amplification in circulating tumor DNA, which has the significant advantage of being noninvasive."

items.statement: Tumor blood vessels are typically normal in architecture with intact basement membranes and pericyte coverage.
items.explanation: False — tumor vessels are abnormal: tortuous, dilated, excessively branched, highly permeable, with discontinuous/absent basement membrane and deficient pericytes, producing chaotic flow, hypoxia, and high interstitial pressure that impairs drug delivery.
items.reference: MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION: "Tumor blood vessels are not normal; they have chaotic architecture and blood flow."

items.statement: Immunosuppressed individuals (e.g., transplant recipients, people with HIV) have an increased risk of developing cancer.
items.explanation: True — increased cancer risk in significantly immunosuppressed individuals (inherited immune defects, transplant immunosuppression, HIV) exemplifies the immune system's critical role in preventing cancer development.
items.reference: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS: "This is exemplified by the increased risk for cancer development in individuals who are significantly immunosuppressed..."

items.assertion: Loss of normal p53 function leaves a cell unprotected against DNA-damaging agents and activated oncogenes.
items.reason: p53 can arrest the cell cycle, direct repair, or trigger apoptosis when DNA damage is sensed.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because p53 normally arrests the cycle/repairs DNA/triggers apoptosis after damage, losing it removes that protection and permits accumulation of DNA damage.
items.reference: CELL CYCLE CHECKPOINTS: "p53 then stops cell cycle progression, directs the synthesis of repair enzymes, or if the damage is too great, initiates apoptosis..."

items.assertion: PARP inhibitors are selectively lethal to BRCA1/2-mutant cancer cells.
items.reason: BRCA-mutant cells cannot repair DNA by homologous recombination, so they become dependent on PARP-mediated single-strand-break repair.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: the loss of homologous recombination makes BRCA-mutant cells reliant on PARP, so PARP inhibition is synthetically lethal in those cells.
items.reference: CANCER-SPECIFIC GENETIC CHANGES AND SYNTHETIC LETHALITY: "cells with mutations in the BRCA1 or BRCA2 tumor-suppressor genes...are unable to repair DNA damage by homologous recombination."

items.assertion: If all malignant cells were dividing without cell death, most patients would reach a lethal tumor burden very quickly.
items.reason: Human tumors grow exponentially, with every daughter cell continuing to divide.
items.explanation: The assertion is true (the hypothetical follows from continuous division), but the reason is false — human tumors grow by Gompertzian, not exponential, kinetics, so not every daughter cell keeps dividing and the growth fraction declines. True assertion + false reason = option 2.
items.reference: DIFFERENCES BETWEEN PEDIATRIC AND ADULT CANCERS: "human tumors grow by Gompertzian kinetics—this means that not every daughter cell produced by a cell division is actively dividing."

items.assertion: Combined inhibition of BRAF and MEK is generally more effective than BRAF inhibition alone in BRAF-mutant melanoma.
items.reason: MEK lies downstream of BRAF in the same signaling pathway, so blocking both more completely suppresses the pathway.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because MEK is downstream of BRAF in the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK pathway, dual inhibition suppresses signaling more completely than BRAF inhibition alone.
items.reference: SIGNAL TRANSDUCTION PATHWAYS IN CANCER CELLS: "combined inhibition of BRAF and MEK is more effective than either alone with activity that extends to BRAF-mutant lung cancer."

items.assertion: Cancer stem cells are relatively resistant to standard chemotherapy and radiation.
items.reason: Cancer stem cells traverse the cell cycle slowly and express high levels of antiapoptotic Bcl-2-family members and drug-efflux pumps.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: slow cycling plus high antiapoptotic and drug-efflux activity make cancer stem cells less vulnerable to therapies that target dividing cells.
items.reference: CANCER STEM CELLS: "Slow cell cycle progression and high levels of expression of antiapoptotic Bcl-2 family members and drug efflux pumps of the MDR family render cancer stem cells less vulnerable to cancer chemotherapy or radiation therapy."

items.assertion: Cancer cells often rely on aerobic glycolysis even though it generates less ATP per glucose than oxidative phosphorylation.
items.reason: Aerobic glycolysis better supplies the biosynthetic building blocks needed to produce new cells.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: despite being less ATP-efficient, the Warburg effect provides building blocks for rapid proliferation, which is why proliferating cancer cells favor it.
items.reference: CANCER METABOLISM: "whereas normal cells utilize oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria...a much more efficient process for generating ATP...but one that does not produce the same level of building blocks needed for new cells."

items.assertion: High interstitial pressure within tumors can interfere with delivery of therapeutics.
items.reason: Tumor vessels are highly permeable with widened junctions and absent basement membrane, and tumors lack functional lymphatics.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: leaky vessels plus absent intratumoral lymphatics raise interstitial pressure, which impedes drug delivery into the tumor.
items.reference: MECHANISMS OF TUMOR VESSEL FORMATION: "together with lack of functional intratumoral lymphatics, causes increased interstitial pressure within the tumor (which also interferes with the delivery of therapeutics to the tumor..."

items.assertion: A single antiangiogenic strategy is likely to suffice for all human cancers.
items.reason: All tumors activate the angiogenic switch using an identical molecular mechanism.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Different tumors use distinct combinations of molecular mechanisms to activate angiogenesis, so it is doubtful a single antiangiogenic strategy will suffice for all cancers. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: ANTIANGIOGENIC THERAPY: "Therefore, it is doubtful that a single antiangiogenic strategy will suffice for all human cancers..."

items.assertion: Downregulation of MHC molecules helps cancer cells evade the immune system.
items.reason: Reduced MHC expression impairs presentation of tumor antigens to T cells.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: by downregulating MHC (and tumor antigens), cancer cells reduce antigen presentation and escape T-cell recognition.
items.reference: TABLE 77-1 (Evasion of the immune system): "Downregulation of MHC class I and II molecules; induction of T-cell tolerance..."

items.assertion: Antigen-negative escape is a recognized mechanism of resistance to CAR-T therapy.
items.reason: Tumor cells that lose expression of the single targeted antigen can outgrow after CAR-T treatment.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: the most common CAR-T resistance mechanism is outgrowth of tumor cells no longer expressing the targeted antigen, prompting strategies that target multiple antigens.
items.reference: EVASION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM BY CANCERS: "mechanisms of resistance have developed, most commonly the outgrowth of tumor cells no longer expressing the antigen."
